← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

Mediterranean

Greek and Roman antiquity — the classical forms that Europe keeps returning to.

The classical world's obsession with the human body, proportion, and idealized form shaped every European revival that came after — Renaissance, Neoclassical, and on. Much of it survives only in fragments.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

Before 1000 BCE

Double-Edged Bannerstone
by Archaic
Tennessee · archaic america · -5800–-4000 · Banded claystone
Notched Ovate Bannerstone
by Archaic
Ohio · archaic america · -5000–-4600 · Banded slate
Kandila (Collared Jar with Conical Foot)
by Master A
Greece, Cycladic · 3000–2950 BCE · marble
Saddle-Face Bannerstone
by Archaic
Indiana · archaic america · -3000–-2000 · Granite
Statuette of a Female Figure
by Cycladic; probably from the island of Keros
Kéa · early cycladic · -2600–-2400 · Marble
Cycladic Figurine
Cycladic · c. 2800–2300 BCE
A pared-down marble female figure with folded arms and minimal features — the form that Brâncuși and Modigliani would later study. Found in Aegean island graves by the hundreds.
Palace of Knossos
Minoan · c. 1900 BCE · Crete
A sprawling Bronze Age palace with running water, frescoes of dolphins and bull-leapers, and a storage maze that may be the origin of the Minotaur myth.
Kamares Ware
Middle Minoan · c. 1800 BCE · Crete
Thin-walled polychrome pottery painted with rippling tendrils and marine forms on a glossy black ground. Named after the sacred cave on Mount Ida where the first examples were found tossed as offerings.
Phaistos Disc
Minoan · c. 1700 BCE · Crete
A fired clay disk stamped with 241 signs in a spiral. Still undeciphered. One of the earliest known uses of movable type — by more than 2,500 years.
Snake Goddess
Minoan faience · c. 1600 BCE · Knossos
A bare-breasted figurine gripping writhing snakes, her flounced skirt falling in tight registers, a feline perched on her head. Found in the palace 'Temple Repository' pits at Knossos, she is probably a household deity or priestess rather than a goddess.
Akrotiri Frescoes
Cycladic/Minoan · c. 1600 BCE · Thera (Santorini)
A Bronze Age town buried by the Thera eruption in meters of ash — its plastered rooms preserved images of saffron-picking girls, fishermen, and a miniature harbor fleet. When excavation began in 1967 the colors were still bright.
Prince of the Lilies
Minoan · c. 1600 BCE · Knossos
A reconstructed relief fresco of a figure crowned with peacock-feathered tiara striding through a field of lilies. Arthur Evans combined three scattered plaster fragments into his imagined 'Priest-King' — later scholars think the pieces likely belong to different figures entirely.
Minoan Female Worshiper
Middle Minoan · c. 1600 BCE · Crete
A small terracotta woman with arms raised in the Minoan worshiper's gesture — palms outward, pressing toward a deity descending from above. Her pose survives from cult, not narrative.
Mask of Agamemnon
Mycenaean · c. 1550 BCE
A beaten-gold funerary mask from Grave Circle A at Mycenae. Schliemann announced he had "looked upon the face of Agamemnon" — though this mask is 300 years too early.
Minoan Petal Bowl
Minoan · c. 1600 BCE · Crete
A shallow stone bowl cut in overlapping petals — Minoan stone vessels, carved from imported Egyptian materials, filled the palaces and ceremonial sites of Knossos.
Bull-Leaping Fresco
Minoan · c. 1500 BCE · Knossos
Three figures vault over a charging bull on the upper east wing of Knossos: the dark-skinned leaper mid-somersault, pale women stationed before and behind. Whether ritual, sport, or myth, it remains the iconic image of Minoan Crete.
Silver Necklace
Aegean, Minoan(?), Bronze Age · c. 1500 BCE · silver
Birdstone
by Archaic
Illinois · archaic america · -1500–-1000 · Stone
Obelisk of Theodosius
Egyptian carved 15th c. BCE, re-erected 390 CE · Istanbul
An Egyptian obelisk Thutmose III set up at Karnak; Theodosius hauled it to Constantinople and planted it on a marble base carved with Byzantine court scenes. Two monuments in one.
Piriform (Pear-Shaped) Jar
Greek, Mycenaean, Late Helladic IIIA1-2b · c. 1400–1300 BCE · ceramic
Hagia Triada Sarcophagus
Late Minoan · c. 1400 BCE · Crete
A painted limestone sarcophagus showing funeral rites on all four sides — a bull sacrifice, women carrying pails of blood, the deceased receiving offerings. Our clearest window onto Minoan ritual.
Treasury of Atreus
Mycenaean · c. 1250 BCE · Mycenae
A tholos tomb — a stone beehive thirteen meters high, held up only by its own corbelled weight. For fifteen centuries, the largest dome in the world.

1000 BCE – 0

Footed Bowl
Cyprus, Cypro-Geometric III · c. 850–750 BCE · white painted ware
Pendant of a Bird (Rooster?)
Northern Greece, Macedonian, Geometric period (900-700 BCE) · 700s–600s BCE · bronze
Altar
by Greek; Thessaly
Greece · geometric · -800–-600 · Bronze
Bird on a Knob
by Greek; Thessaly
Greece · geometric · -800–-600 · Bronze
Geometric Horse Pyxis (Cylindrical Lidded Box)
Greek, Attic, Late Geometric la · c. 760–750 BCE · ceramic
Geometric Horse Pyxis (Cylindrical Box)
Greek, Attic · c. 760–750 BCE · ceramic
Geometric Horse Pyxis (Cylindrical Lid)
Greek, Attic · c. 760–750 BCE · ceramic
Dipylon Amphora
Geometric Greek · c. 750 BCE
A grave marker nearly five feet tall, painted with stylized mourners in a prothesis scene. The moment figurative imagery returns to Greek pottery after five centuries of pure geometry.
Geometric Horse Statuette
Archaic Greek · c. 750 BCE
A small bronze horse from the Geometric period — long-legged, long-necked, stylized almost into a diagram. Bronze horses were dedicated at Olympia by the thousand.
Statuette of a Horse
Greek, Geometric, probably Corinthian · 750–700 BCE · bronze
Geometric Swan Pitcher
Archaic Greek · Swan Painter · c. 740 BCE
An Attic pitcher painted with a frieze of stylized swans between lines of key-pattern — the Geometric style that preceded figural Greek painting, found mostly on grave offerings.
Lebes (Stemmed Bowl with Lid)
by Etruscan; possibly Vulci
Etruria · etruscan · -725–-700 · terracotta
Woman's Belt Hanger (Zone)
Greece, Geometric period · c. 725–675 BCE · bronze
Prothesis Amphora
Archaic Greek · c. 720 BCE
A large neck-handled amphora showing a laying-out of the dead (prothesis) — the corpse on a bier, mourners raising their hands. Grave markers in themselves; they held offerings for the dead inside.
Black-Figure Kotyle (Drinking Cup): Animal Friezes
by Shoe Lane Painter
Greek, Corinthian · 600s–500s BCE, with modern painting and incision · ceramic
Protocorinthian (Early Black-Figure) Aryballos (Oil Flask): Animals
Greek, Corinthian · c. 650–640 BCE · ceramic
Lady of Auxerre
Archaic Greek · c. 630 BCE
A small limestone standing female figure in a belted chiton, right hand to breast. One of the earliest Greek monumental sculptures — the "Daedalic" style, before the Kouros.
Aryballos (Container for Oil)
by Etrusco-Corinthian; Etruria, Italy
Greece · etruscan · -625–-600 · terracotta
Aryballos (Cointainer for Oil)
by Greek; Corinth
Ancient Greece · greek · -625–-600 · terracotta
Polychrome Oinochoe (Wine Jug): Deer and Lions (?)
Etruscan (Etrusco-Corinthian), probably made at Vulci · c. 600–575 BCE · ceramic
Black-Figure Neck-Amphora (Storage Vessel): Animal Friezes
by St. Louis Painter
Etruscan (Etrusco-Corinthian) · c. 600–580 BCE · ceramic
Corinthian Bull-Shaped Aryballos
Corinthian Greece · c. 600 BCE · ceramic
An oil flask shaped as a bull, once filled with olive oil for the gymnasium — Corinth exported animal-shaped vessels across the Mediterranean in the orientalizing century.
Amphoriskos (Container for Oil)
by Greek; Corinth
Corinth · corinthian (pottery style) · -600–-575 · terracotta
Stater: Lion (obverse); Incuse Punches (reverse)
Greek, minted at Miletos (Ionia) · 600–550 BCE · Electrum (gold-silver alloy)
Female Statuette
Italy, Etruscan, Archaic period · 600–480 BCE · bronze
Stater: Three incuse punches (reverse)
Greek, minted at Miletos (Ionia) · 600–550 BCE · Electrum (gold-silver alloy)
Stater: Lion, crouching, l., looking behind, within frame (obverse)
Greek, minted at Miletos (Ionia) · 600–550 BCE · Electrum (gold-silver alloy)
Statuette of a Goddess
by Greek; Boeotia
Dhodhóni · archaic (greek) · -600–-580 · terracotta
Black-Figure Kotyle (Drinking Cup): Chain of Women
by Patras Painter
Greek, Corinthian · c. 590–560 BCE, with modern repainting and reincising · ceramic
New York Kouros
Archaic Greek · c. 590 BCE
One of the earliest freestanding Greek marble nudes, Attic. Still stiff with Egyptian influence — striding left foot, clenched fists — but the archaic smile has arrived.
Heron-Shaped Figure Vase (Oil Vessel)
by Robertson's Group
East Greek, probably Milesian · c. 580 BCE · ceramic
Etruscan Kouros
Etruscan · 6th c. BCE
A small votive bronze of a nude striding youth — the Greek kouros form received in Etruria. Etruscan bronzes were famous across the Mediterranean.
Black-Figure Olpe (Wine Jug): Rooster
Greek, Middle/Late Corinthian · c. 575 BCE · ceramic
Siana Cup (Black-Figure Kylix [Drinking Cup]): Horseman and Warriors
Greek, Attic · 575–550 BCE · ceramic
François Vase
by Kleitias & Ergotimos
Attic black-figure · c. 570 BCE · signed Kleitias/Ergotimos
A volute krater dense with 270 named figures in six stacked registers — Achilles ambushing Troilus, the marriage of Peleus and Thetis, the Calydonian boar hunt. Found in fragments in an Etruscan tomb near Chiusi in 1844.
Moschophoros (Calf Bearer)
Archaic Greek · c. 560 BCE · Acropolis
A man carries a calf to sacrifice; both smile — the archaic grin. His tunic is incised rather than carved, an early convention already on its way out.
Black-Figure Oinochoe (Wine Jug): Boxers
by Class of London B 524
Greek, Attic · c. 550–540 BCE · ceramic
Torso of a Kouros
Greece, Archaic period · c. 550 BCE · marble
Situla
Etruscan · c. 525–450 BCE · bronze
Pyxis (Container for Personal Objects)
by Greek; Athens
Greece · greek · -550–-500 · terracotta
Belly-Amphora (Storage Jar)
by Attributed to the Painter of Berlin 1686 or the Painter of Tarquinia RC 3984
Athens · archaic (greek) · -550–-540 · terracotta
Oinochoe (Pitcher)
by Etruscan
Etruria · etruscan · -550–-500 · terracotta
Stemmed Kyathos (Drinking Cup)
by Etruscan; probably Vulci
Vulci · early etruscan · -550–-525 · terracotta
Statuette of a Young Spearman
Etruscan, Archaic Period · late 500s BCE · Bronze
Fibula
Etruscan · c. 500 BCE · bronze
Black-Figure Amphora (Type B; Storage Vessel): Men and Horses
Greek, Attic · c. 540 BCE · ceramic
Heraion of Samos
Archaic Greek · 6th c. BCE
Sanctuary of Hera near her birthplace. Four successive temples built on the same footprint; the fourth was the largest Greek temple ever begun — never finished. One column standing.
Kylix (Drinking Cup)
by Greek; Athens
Polis · archaic (greek) · -540–-530 · terracotta
Black-Figure Amphora (Type B; Storage Vessel): Battling Warriors (A); Dionysos and Satyrs (B)
by The Bateman Group
Greek, Attic · c. 530–520 BCE · ceramic
Black-Figure Nikosthenic Amphora (Storage Vessel): Dancing Youths; Sphinxes and Lions; Satyrs and Maenads
by Nikosthenes
Greek, Attic · c. 530–510 BCE · ceramic
Fragment from Black-Figure Amphora (Storage Vessel): Musicians
Greek, Attic · c. 530–520 BCE · ceramic
Black-Figure Trefoil Oinochoe (Wine Jug): Europa on Bull
by Class of Vatican 440
Greek, Attic · c. 530 BCE · ceramic
Kroisos Kouros
Archaic Greek · c. 530 BCE
A young man stepping forward, hands at his sides, slight smile — the archaic formula. Found on a grave at Anavyssos, one leg at a time.
Peplos Kore
Archaic Greek · c. 530 BCE · Athens
A marble girl from the Acropolis, once brightly painted — red for her peplos, yellow for her hair. Arm broken off; traces of paint and pigment still cling in her carved hair.
Amphora (Storage Jar)
by Attributed to the Ivy Leaf Group
Etruria · etruscan · -530–-520 · terracotta
Black-Figure Neck-Amphora of Panathenaic Shape (Storage Vessel)
Greek, Attic · 525–500 BCE · ceramic
Black-Figure Neck-Amphora (Storage Vessel): Departing Warriors (A); Dionysos and Satyrs (B)
Greek, Attic · c. 520–510 BCE · ceramic
Black-Figure Neck-Amphora (Storage Vessel): Departing Warrior with Chariot (A); Battling Warriors (B)
Greek, Attic · c. 520–510 BCE · ceramic
Fragment from Black-Figure Neck-Amphora (Storage Vessel): Satyr and Horse
Greek, Attic · c. 520–510 BCE · ceramic
Fragment from Black-Figure Neck-Amphora of Panathenaic Shape (Storage Vessel): Apollo and Zeus
by Antimenes Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 520 BCE · ceramic
Fragment from Black-Figure Neck-Amphora of Panathenaic Shape (Storage Vessel): Apollo with Lyre
by Antimenes Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 520 BCE · ceramic
Red-Figure Eye Cup: Warriors (A), Musician and Youths (B)
by Psiax
Greek, Attic · c. 520 BCE · ceramic
Candelabrum Stand of a Dancing Maenad
Etruscan · 525–500 BCE · bronze
Vase
by Antimenes Painter
Attica · ca. 525 BC
Antefix with Satyr Face
Greek/South Italian, Taranto · c. 525–480 BCE · terracotta
Black-Figure Eye Cup: Iris and Satyrs (A, B); Gorgoneion (I)
Greek, Attic · c. 520–510 BCE · ceramic
Black-Figure Hydria (Water Vessel): Frontal Quadriga (Body); Theseus and Minotaur (Shoulder)
by Antimenes Painter
Greece, Attic · c. 520 BCE · ceramic
Sarcophagus of the Spouses
Etruscan · c. 520 BCE · Italy
A painted terracotta couple reclining together on their tomb lid — smiling, touching, treated as equals. Unthinkable in contemporary Greek or Egyptian funerary art.
Black-Figure Dinos (Mixing Vessel): Warships (Int.); Heroic Scenes (Top)
by Antimenes Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 520–515 BCE · ceramic
Statuette of a Youth
Italy, Etruscan, Archaic Period · c. 520–500 BCE · bronze
Euphronios Krater
by Euphronios
Archaic Greek · Euphronios &amp; Euxitheos · c. 515 BCE
A red-figure calyx-krater showing Hypnos and Thanatos carrying Sarpedon's body. Looted from an Etruscan tomb; caught up in a 30-year dispute; finally returned to Italy in 2008.
Herakles and the Nemean Lion Amphora
Archaic Greek · Painter of Berlin 1899 · c. 515 BCE
A black-figure neck-amphora showing the first labor — Herakles wrestling the Nemean lion bare-handed, since blades couldn't pierce its skin. Opposite side: Dionysos with satyrs.
Hydria (Water Jar)
by Attributed to a painter of the Leagros group
Athens · archaic (greek) · -515–-500 · terracotta
Black-Figure Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Seated Men, Warriors and Horse
by Leagros Group
Greek, Attic · c. 510–500 BCE · ceramic
Apollo of Veii
by attributed to Vulca
Etruscan · c. 510 BCE · Veii
A full-size polychrome terracotta from the roofline of the Portonaccio sanctuary, striding forward to contest Herakles for a stolen deer. Its broad smile and swinging drapery put Etruscan sculpture at parity with anything being made in archaic Greece.
Pelike (Storage Jar)
by Greek; Athens
Greece · greek · -510–-500 · terracotta
Statuette of Herakles
Greek · 510–500 BCE · bronze (solid cast)
Black-Figure Loutrophoros-Amphora (Ritual Water Vessel): Prothesis (Laying out of Corpse), Mourners
Greek, Attic · c. 500 BCE · ceramic
Black-Figure White-Ground Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Horseman, Horse between Warriors, Dog
by Edinburgh Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 500 BCE · ceramic
Black-Figure Kantharos (Drinking Cup): Lizards Flanking Tree (A); Ducks (B)
Greek, Boiotian · c. 500 BCE · ceramic
Temple of Aphaia
Late Archaic Greek · c. 500 BCE · Aegina
A limestone temple whose pedimental sculptures, now in Munich, capture the hinge between Archaic stiffness and Classical naturalism — look at the east and west together to see a style changing.
Roman Forum
Roman Republic → Imperial · 7th c. BCE – 4th c. CE
The civic heart of ancient Rome — law courts, speaker's platforms, temples, triumphal arches, all crammed into a low strip between the Palatine and Capitoline hills.
Etruscan Tinia
Etruscan · c. 500 BCE
A small bronze of Tinia, the Etruscan Zeus, thunderbolt raised. Etruscans named their pantheon after themselves; their Greek-looking gods carried Etruscan names.
Black-Figure White-Ground Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Athena between Cocks and Hieropoioi (Officials)
by Athena Painter
Greek, Attic · 500–485 BCE · Ceramic
Mastoid (Drinking Cup) with Handles
by Attributed to the Manner of the Haimon Painter
Athens · attic · -500–-450 · terracotta
Foot of a Cista (Storage Box)
by Etruscan; probably Vulci
Vulci · etruscan · -500–-450 · Bronze
Votive (Gift) in the Shape of a Woman's Head
by Etruscan; possibly Veii
Veio · early etruscan · -500 · terracotta
Pawing Bull
South Italy, Lucania, Greek · 500–475 BCE · bronze
Mastoid (Drinking Cup)
by Attributed to the Caylus Painter
Athens · attic · -500–-480 · terracotta
Diobol (Coin) Depicting an Ear of Grain
by Greek; minted in Metapontum, Italy
Metaponto · archaic (greek) · -500–-473 · Silver
Pendant in the Shape of a Head
by Phoenician; probably from the Eastern Mediterranean, or possibly Carthage
Carthage · phoenician · -500–-201 · Glass
Miniature Sword
Etruscan · 500–450 BCE · bronze
Statuette of a Warrior
Etruscan · 500–450 BCE · bronze
Stater (Coin) Depicting a Tripod
by Greek; Crotone, Italy
Crotone · archaic (greek) · -500–-480 · Silver
Coin Depicting Pegasus
by Greek; minted in Corinth
Levkás · archaic (greek) · -500–-450 · Silver
Red-Figure Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Athena Slaying Giant (body); Satyr between Maenads (shoulder)
by Berlin Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 490 BCE · ceramic
Fragmentary Red-Figure Kylix (Drinking Cup): Warrior Sacrificing Ram
Greek, Attic · c. 490–480 BCE · ceramic
White-Ground Lekythos by Douris
by Douris
Classical Greek · Douris · c. 490 BCE
An oil vessel painted by Douris with Atalanta pursued by Erotes — the huntress heroine running among winged loves. White-ground lekythoi were funerary, often gifts at a grave.
Tetradrachm (Coin) Depicting the Goddess Athena
by Greek, minted in Athens
Athens · archaic (greek) · -490 · Silver
Red-Figure Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Warrior Cutting Hair
by Oionokles Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 480–470 BCE · ceramic
Red-Figure Ram-Head Rhyton (Drinking Vessel): Symposiasts; Satyr and Maenad
by Brygos Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 480–470 BCE · ceramic
Tetradrachm (Coin) Depicting Quadriga with Bearded Charioteer
by Greek, minted in Syracuse, Sicily
Ancient Greece · archaic (greek) · -485–-478 · Silver
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying Biga with Mules
by Greek; minted in Messana, Sicily, Italy
Ancient Greece · archaic (greek) · -484–-476 · Silver
Kritios Boy
Early Classical Greek · c. 480 BCE
The hinge between Archaic and Classical — the first marble body where weight shifts onto one leg (contrapposto), giving the figure life.
Tomb of the Leopards
Etruscan painted tomb · c. 480 BCE · Tarquinia
A small banquet chamber whose back wall shows two leopards flanking a lintel, below which men recline on couches while flute and lyre players serenade them. The brilliant red-and-black palette and easy joy have no parallel in surviving Greek painting.
Tomb of the Diver
Greek colonial · c. 480 BCE · Paestum
The only known example of Greek figural tomb painting from the classical period. A young man dives into water beneath a lone tree — possibly a metaphor for the leap into the afterlife; the side walls show a genial symposium underway.
Apollo of Piombino
Archaic Greek · c. 480 BCE
A life-sized bronze Apollo dredged up by fishermen off Piombino in 1832. Inscribed in silver with an Etruscan workshop's signature — a Greek form made on Italian soil.
Kantharos (Wine Cup) in the Shape of a Female Head
by Attributed to the London Class
Athens · attic · -480 · terracotta
Stamnos (Mixing Jar)
by Attributed to Syriskos
Athens · attic · -480–-470 · terracotta
Charioteer of Delphi
Early Classical · c. 478 BCE
One of the few surviving Greek bronzes. His calm expression, inset with glass and copper eyes, is a moment in a victory lap — all force held still.
Red-Figure Column Krater (Mixing Vessel): Komos (Revel)
by Pig Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 470–460 BCE · ceramic
Fragment from Red-Figure Cup: Woman
by Telephos Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 470–460 BCE · ceramic
Fragment from Red-Figure Kylix: Nereus(?) and Nereid
by Pistoxenos Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 470–460 BCE · ceramic
Red-Figure Column Krater (Mixing Vessel): Apollo and Goddesses with Chariot (A); Komos (Revel) (B)
by Cleveland Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 470–460 BCE · ceramic
Red-Figure Janiform Kantharos (Two-Sided Head-Shaped Drinking Cup): Satyr and African
by Class K
Greek, Attic · c. 470–460 BCE · ceramic
Ludovisi Throne
Greek · c. 470 BCE
A marble three-sided relief of uncertain function (a throne? an altar?). On its main panel, Aphrodite rising from the sea, draped in a wet gown that might as well not be there.
Temple of Zeus at Olympia
Classical Greek · c. 470 BCE
Once held the chryselephantine Zeus — one of the Seven Wonders of the ancient world, carved by Phidias. The temple fell in an earthquake; the Zeus was hauled to Constantinople and burned.
Etruscan Mirror: Eos and Memnon
Etruscan · c. 470 BCE · bronze
A polished-bronze mirror back engraved with Eos carrying the dead body of her son Memnon — the Etruscans loved Greek myth but gave the figures Etruscan names: Thesan for Eos, Memnun for Memnon.
Kottabos Element of a Dancing Satyr
Etruscan · 470–450 BCE · bronze
Hand Mirror
by Etruscan; probably Vulci
Vulci · early etruscan · -470–-450 · Bronze
Artemision Bronze
Classical Greek · c. 460 BCE
Pulled from a Roman shipwreck off Cape Artemision. Either Zeus throwing a thunderbolt or Poseidon throwing a trident — his missing weapon makes it impossible to say.
Column-Krater (Mixing Bowl)
by Attributed to a Member of the Earlier Mannerist Group
Athens · attic · -460 · terracotta
Mug
by Greek; Athens
Greece · greek · -460 · terracotta
Mirror Support: Athlete
South Italy, Locri (?), Greek · 460–450 BCE · bronze
Stemless Kylix (Drinking Cup)
by Greek; Athens
Cales · greek · -460–-450 · terracotta
Riace Bronzes
Classical Greek · c. 460–450 BCE · Italy
Two life-size bronze warriors pulled from the sea off Calabria in 1972. Their teeth are silver, their eyelashes hammered copper.
Discobolus
by Myron
Classical Greek · c. 450 BCE (Roman copy)
Myron's discus thrower — the original bronze is lost; only Roman marble copies survive. A moment just before release, coiled and still.
Capitoline Wolf
Etruscan or Medieval bronze · 5th c. BCE or 13th c. CE
The she-wolf nursing Romulus and Remus — emblem of Rome. Once thought ancient Etruscan; recent analysis suggests a medieval re-casting.
Lekanis (Covered Dish)
by Greek; Athens
Greece · greek · -450–-430 · terracotta
Fragment of a Reclining Male Figure
Greek/South Italian, Taranto (ancient Taras) · 450–350 BCE · terracotta
Doryphoros
by Polykleitos
Classical Greek · Polykleitos · c. 440 BCE
The "Spear Bearer" — a treatise on ideal human proportion made in marble. Polykleitos wrote a book called the Canon about it; only this statue survives.
Parthenon Frieze
Classical Greek · Pheidias workshop · c. 440 BCE
A continuous band 160 meters long, showing the Panathenaic procession — horses, musicians, elders, gods receiving the city's tribute. Half survives in the British Museum, half in Athens.
Temple of Poseidon at Sounion
Classical Greek · c. 440 BCE · Attica
White marble columns on a cape where the sea drops 60 meters away on three sides. Byron carved his name into one of the columns in 1810.
Tazza
Attica · ca. 440 BC
Red-Figure Column Krater (Mixing Vessel): Satyrs Pursuing Maenads
by Naples Painter
Greek, Attic · 440–430 BCE · ceramic
The Parthenon
Classical Greek · 447–432 BCE · Athens
Temple to Athena on the Acropolis. The columns lean inward and swell slightly in the middle — optical corrections so they look perfectly straight.
Propylaea of the Acropolis
Classical Greek · Mnesicles · 437–432 BCE · Athens
The ceremonial gateway to the Acropolis. An unfinished masterpiece — the Peloponnesian War stopped construction; beam sockets on the unfinished wings still wait for marble that never came.
Fragment from Red-Figure Oinochoe (Wine Jug) or Hydria (Water Jar): Woman Holding Box
by Shuvalov Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 425–420 BCE · ceramic
Red-Figure Squat Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Birth of Erichthonios
by Meidias Painter
Greek, Attic · c. 420–410 BCE · ceramic, with gilding
White-Ground Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Youths at Tomb
by Group R
Greek, Attic · c. 420–400 BCE · ceramic
Torso From a Doll
by Greek
Tanágra · greek · -425–-300 · terracotta
Nike of Paionios
by Paionios
Classical Greek · Paionios · c. 420 BCE · Olympia
The Messenians and Naupactians dedicated her at Olympia after a victory over Sparta. Nike lands on a plinth, wind pressing drapery against her body — 170 years before the Winged Victory of Samothrace.
Erechtheion
Classical Greek · 421–406 BCE · Athens
Temple on the sacred north side of the Acropolis. Six marble maidens (the Caryatids) hold up the porch in place of columns.
Skyphos (Drinking Cup)
by Greek; Athens
Cales · greek · -410–-400 · terracotta
Red-Figure Squat Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Eros and Woman
Greek, Attic · c. 400–380 BCE · ceramic
Red-Figure Calyx-Krater (Mixing Vessel): Medea in Chariot (A); Telephos with Baby Orestes (B)
by Policoro Painter
South Italian, Lucanian · c. 400 BCE · ceramic
Chimera of Arezzo
Etruscan · c. 400 BCE
Bronze of the mythical lion-goat-snake beast, wounded and snarling. Found in 1553 and immediately cleaned up by Cellini; the inscription on its leg is Etruscan, not Latin.
Mars of Todi
Etruscan · c. 400 BCE
A life-sized bronze warrior, once holding a spear, a libation bowl in the surviving hand. Umbrian workmanship in Greek-classicizing style, inscribed with an Umbrian-language dedication.
Etruscan Sleep and Death Handle
Etruscan · 4th c. BCE
A bronze handle in the form of winged Hypnos (Sleep) and Thanatos (Death) — twin brothers carrying a dead youth between them. Cast for the lid of a cylindrical cista cosmetic box.
Vessel Ornament of Banqueter
Etruscan · probably 400–375 BCE · bronze
Vessel Ornament of Reclining Pipes Player
Etruscan · probably 400–375 BCE · bronze
Vessel Ornament of Banqueter Holding an Omphalos
Etruscan · probably 400–375 BCE · bronze
Pair of Earrings or Hair Ringlets with Ram Head
Greece, Hellenistic period · 400s BCE · gold
Calyx Krater (Mixing Bowl)
by Attributed to The Perugia Painter, now combined with the Arnò Painter
Basilicata · lucanian · -400–-380 · terracotta
Fish Plate
by Greek; Athens
Athens · greek · -400–-370 · terracotta
Vessel Ornament of Reclining Lyre-player
Etruscan · probably 400–375 BCE · bronze with gilding traces
Fibula (Pin)
Etruscan · 300s BCE · gold
Statuette of a Siren
by Greek; possibly Myrina, Lemnos
Greece · greek · -400–-100 · terracotta
Hair Ringlet with Ram Head
Greece, Hellenistic period · 400s BCE · gold
Bovine Finial
Greece or Turkey, Thracian · 400–300 BCE · gold, hammered
Male Votive Figure
Etruscan · 300s BCE · bronze
Frog-Shaped Guttus (Oil Flask)
South Italian, Apulian · 300s BCE · ceramic
Red-Figure Bell-Krater (Mixing Vessel): Bust of Dionysos and Comic Actors (A); Dionysos, Satyr, Maenad (B)
by Choregos Painter
South Italian, Apulian · c. 390–380 BCE · ceramic
Hecte: Head of Omphale (obverse); Incuse Square (reverse)
Greek, minted at Phokaia (Ionia) · 387–326 BCE · electrum (gold-silver alloy)
Hecte: Head of Omphale (obverse)
Greek, minted at Phokaia (Ionia) · 387–326 BCE · electrum (gold-silver alloy)
Figure of a Youth from a Funerary Stele (Monument)
by Greek; Athens
Greece · greek · -380 · Marble
Inlay: Head of a King
Egypt, Greco-Roman period (332 BCE–395 CE), Ptolemaic dynasty (305–30 BCE) · 380–246 BCE · red jasper
Red-Figure Bell Krater (Mixing Vessel): Aphrodite and Eros
by Graz Painter
South Italian, Apulian · c. 370–360 BCE · ceramic
Aphrodite of Knidos
by Praxiteles
Classical Greek · Praxiteles · c. 360 BCE
The first free-standing nude female figure in Greek sculpture — scandalous enough that men climbed her sanctuary walls at night. Only copies survive.
Tetradrachm (Coin) Depicting the God Zeus
by Greek
Kavála · hellenistic · -359–-336 · Silver
Red-Figure Situla (Deep Bucket): Bellerophon Slaying Chimaera (A); Dionysos, Maenads, Eros (B)
by Group of the Dublin Situlae
South Italian, Apulian · c. 350 BCE · ceramic
Lady of Elche
Iberian · c. 4th c. BCE · Spain
A limestone bust with enormous spoked headdress-wheels over the ears. Iberian, not Greek — the style belongs to a world that left no writing to explain itself.
Mausoleum at Halicarnassus
Greek · c. 350 BCE · Turkey
Tomb of the Carian satrap Mausolus, so grand that his name became the word for any lavish tomb. Wonder of the ancient world; shaken down by earthquakes; its fragments in the British Museum.
Temple of Apollo at Delphi
Classical Greek · 4th c. BCE · Delphi
The seat of the Oracle. Inscribed on its wall: "Know thyself." Six of the original 38 Doric columns still stand at the edge of the sacred precinct.
Red-Figure Squat Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Female Head
South Italian, Apulian · 350–300 BCE · ceramic
Red-Figure Cow-Head Rhyton (Drinking Horn): Seated Woman
South Italian, Apulian, Tarentine · c. 340 BCE · ceramic
Red-Figure Oinochoe (Wine Jug): Eros and Woman
by Cleveland Group
South Italian, Apulian · c. 340–320 BCE · ceramic
Hermes of Praxiteles
by Praxiteles
Classical Greek · c. 340 BCE · Olympia
Hermes leans against a tree holding the infant Dionysus, swinging a bunch of grapes just out of reach. Found in 1877 in the ruins of the Temple of Hera — one of the rare original Greek marbles from a named master.
Tetradrachm (Coin) Depicting the Hero Herakles with the Features of Alexander the Great
by Greek, minted in Amphipolis, Macedonia
Amfípolis · greek · -336–-323 · Silver
Hippopotamus
Italy, Rome, Greco-Roman Period · 332 BCE–395 CE · bronze, solid cast
Apoxyomenos
by Lysippos
Classical Greek · Lysippos · c. 330 BCE
An athlete scraping oil from his arm. The Roman marble copy stands in for a lost bronze original by Alexander the Great's favorite sculptor.
Mirror with Engraved Scene: Two Female Figures
Etruscan · 330–300 BCE · bronze
Mirror with Engraved Scene: Man Threatening Child (Telephos and Orestes?)
Italy, Etruscan, probably Praenestine · c. 330–300 BCE · bronze
Box Mirror with Head of Athena (lid)
Greece, Hellenistic period · 330–270 BCE · bronze, partially gilt
Box Mirror with Head of Athena
Greece, Hellenistic period · 330–270 BCE · bronze, partially gilt
Statuette of a Seated Girl
by Greek; Athens
Athens · hellenistic · -330–-320 · terracotta
Red-Figure Kantharos (Drinking Cup): Female Heads
by Prague Group
South Italian, Apulian · 330–320 BCE · ceramic
Knob-Handled Patera (Dish)
by Attributed to the Baltimore Painter
Apulia · apulian · -330–-320 · terracotta
Box Mirror with Head of Athena (disk)
Greece, Hellenistic period · 330–270 BCE · bronze, partially gilt
Female Figurine
Greece, Tanagra (Boeotia) · 330–200 BCE · molded terracotta, painted
Stater: Head of Athena (obverse); Nike (reverse)
Greek, Macedonian, minted at Salamis (Cyprus) · c. 325–275 BCE · gold
Stater: Head of Athena (obverse)
Greek, Macedonian, minted at Salamis (Cyprus) · c. 325–275 BCE · gold
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying Alexander the Great as the Hero Herakles
by Greek; minted in Sidon, ancient Phoenicia
Macedonia · hellenistic · -323–-317 · Silver
Alexander Sarcophagus
Hellenistic · c. 320 BCE · Sidon
Pentelic marble sarcophagus carved with Alexander hunting lions and battling Persians — the reliefs still holding flecks of original paint. Not Alexander's tomb: it was made for a Sidonian king he had favored.
Kantharos (Wine Cup)
by Greek; Apulia, Italy
Apulia · hellenistic · -310–-280 · Terracotta with traces of gilding
Tetradrachm (Coin) Depicting the Goddess Persephone
by Greek, minted in Syracuse, Sicily
Syracuse · hellenistic · -310–-307 · Silver
Gnathian Ribbed Oinochoe (Wine Jug): Masks, Dancer, and Flute-Player
South Italian, Apulian · c. 300–275 BCE · ceramic
Cat Coffin
Egypt, Greco-Roman period (332 BCE–395 CE), Ptolemaic dynasty (305–30 BCE) · 305–30 BCE · bronze, hollow cast
Capitoline Brutus
Roman Republican bronze · 4th–3rd c. BCE
A stern bronze head long identified with Lucius Junius Brutus, the founder of the Republic. Inlaid ivory and glass eyes stare straight past the viewer — the Roman virtue of gravitas given a face.
Tanagra Figurine
Hellenistic · 4th–3rd c. BCE
Terracotta figurines of ordinary women, fashionable citizens, children — bright-painted, cheap, mass-produced from molds. The first folk-class sculpture of the classical world.
Patera Support: Lasa
Etruscan · 300–175 BCE · bronze with silver inlays
Architectural Relief Depicting the Gigantomachy (Battle Between Gods and Giants)
by Etruscan
Etruria · hellenistic etruscan · -300–-101 · Terracotta and pigment
Mirror with a Handle in the Form of a Female Figure
by Etruscan
Etruria · etruscan · -300–-201 · Bronze
Head of Alexander the Great
Greece, Alexandria, Hellenistic · 300–200 BCE · marble
Earring with Dolphin Head Finial
by Greek, Hellenistic
Ancient Greece · hellenistic · -300–-101 · Gold
Coin Depicting a Horse and Palm Tree
by Carthaginian
Carthage · phoenician · -300–-201 · Bronze
Miniature Pyxis (Container for Personal Objects)
by Greek; probably Campania, Italy
Cales · greek · -300–-270 · terracotta
Relief of Striding Warriors
Greek/South Italian, Taranto (ancient Taras) · 300–200 BCE · limestone
Head of a Woman
Greece, Hellenistic period · 300–200 BCE · marble
Head of Herakles Refashioned as a Weight
Greece, Hellenistic period · 300–200 BCE · bronze filled with lead
Figurine of a Lady
Greece, Tanagra · 200s BCE · terracotta
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying Alexander the Great
by Greek; minted in Ephesus, Asia Minor
Ephesus · hellenistic · -297–-281 · Silver
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying Demetrios I of Macedonia
by Greek; minted in Amphipolis, Macedonia
Macedonia · hellenistic · -289–-288 · Silver
Stater (Coin) Depicting the Siren Parthenope
by Greek, minted in Neapolis
Kavála · hellenistic · -280–-241 · Silver
Coin Depicting the God Zeus Ammon
by Greek; Cyrenaicia, North Africa
Shahhat · phoenician · -247–-221 · Bronze
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying Philetairos of Pergamon
by Greek; minted in Pergamon, Mysia, Asia Minor
Bergama · hellenistic · -241–-197 · Silver
Coin Depicting Cow Suckling Calf
by Greek; Illyria
Illyria · greek · -229–-100 · Silver
Dying Gaul
Hellenistic · c. 230–220 BCE (Roman copy)
A defeated Celt collapsing on his shield, wound dripping. Pergamene art showed the enemy as human — a compliment paid in marble, after the kill.
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying King Antiochus III The Great
by Greek; probably minted in Antioch
Ancient Near East · hellenistic · -223–-187 · Silver
Barberini Faun
Hellenistic · c. 220 BCE
A sleeping satyr sprawled drunk on a rock, legs splayed, utterly without dignity. Excavated from Hadrian's mausoleum moat — perhaps thrown over the walls during a siege.
Octodrachm: Head of Arsinoe II (obverse); Double Cornucopia (reverse)
Greek, Ptolemaic, minted at Alexandreia (Egypt), reign of Ptolemy V Epiphanes or Ptolemy VI Philometor · 205–145 BCE · gold
Winged Victory of Samothrace
Hellenistic · c. 200 BCE
Nike landing on the prow of a ship, wind pressing her drapery against her body. One of the most physically dynamic pieces of ancient sculpture.
Incense Burner
Etruscan · 100s BCE · bronze
Alabastron (Container for Scented Oil)
by Late Hellenistic or early Roman; eastern Mediterranean
Eastern Mediterranean Region · roman (ancient, style or period) · -200–1 · Glass
Mule-Head Attachment for a Couch
Greece, Hellenistic period · 200–100 BCE · bronze
Tritoness or Scylla Relief Applique
Greece, Hellenistic period · late 200s BCE · bronze with copper inlays
Pendant Depicting Apollo
by Greek
Greece · greek · -200–-1 · terracotta
Coin Depicting the God Asklepios (?)
by Greek
Ancient Greece · late hellenistic · -200–-133 · Bronze
Torque
Greece, late Hellenistic period · 200–1 BCE · silver
Amphoriskos
Greece, late Hellenistic period · 200–1 BCE · silver
Pergamon Altar
Hellenistic · c. 180–160 BCE · Pergamon
A colossal marble frieze of the gods fighting the giants — deep-cut writhing bodies that may have inspired Michelangelo. The whole structure was hauled to Berlin in 1880.
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying Demetrios I Soter
by Greek; minted in Apamea
Syria · hellenistic · -162–-150 · Silver
Tetradrachm: Macedonian Shield with Head of Artemis (obverse); Club (reverse)
Greek, Macedonian, minted at Amphipolis (Macedonia), under Roman control · 158–150 BCE · silver
House of the Faun
Roman · 2nd c. BCE · Pompeii
One of the largest residences in Pompeii, three acres across. Its atrium held the bronze dancing faun that gave it the name; the floor held the Alexander Mosaic.
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying King Antiochus VII Euergetes Sidetes
by Greek; probably minted in Antioch
Ancient Near East · hellenistic · -138–-129 · Silver
Coin Depicting the God Zeus and Consort (?)
by Phoenician
Ancient Mediterranean · phoenician · -137–-127 · Bronze
Tetradrachm (Coin) Depicting a Cista with Snake
by Greek; minted in Pergamon, Asia Minor
Ancient Greece · greek · -133–-67 · Silver
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying Demetrius II Nikator of Syria
by Greek, minted in Tyre
Tyre · greek · -130–-129 · Silver
Nanny Goat
Greece, Hellenistic period · late 200s BCE · bronze
Antikythera Mechanism
Hellenistic · c. 150–100 BCE · Greece
A corroded bronze device pulled from a Roman shipwreck in 1901. CT scans finally revealed: a geared orrery predicting sun, moon, and five planets. No surviving match until the Renaissance.
Venus de Milo
Hellenistic Greek · c. 130–100 BCE
Marble Aphrodite found on the island of Milos in 1820. The missing arms are part of her fame — we don't know what she was doing.
Boxer at Rest
Hellenistic · c. 100 BCE
A bronze boxer after a fight — cauliflower ears, bloodied lip, exhausted. One of the few surviving Greek originals, not a later copy.
Alexander Mosaic
Roman copy of Hellenistic painting · c. 100 BCE · Pompeii
Alexander charging Darius at Issus, rendered in a million tiny tesserae. The lost original oil painting was probably by Philoxenos of Eretria.
Borghese Gladiator
by Agasias of Ephesus
c. 100 BCE · Marble · Sully Wing
Not actually a gladiator — a warrior fighting a mounted opponent, arm raised with a (lost) shield. A tour de force of anatomical study; every muscle is taut with the effort of the moment before impact.
L'Arringatore
Etruscan-Roman · c. 100 BCE
A bronze orator raising his hand to speak, in a senator's toga. Inscribed in Etruscan — one of the last great Etruscan bronzes, on the cusp of full Romanization.
Statue of Young Dionysos
by Hellenistic or Roman; Eastern Mediterranean
Eastern Mediterranean Region · hellenistic · -100–100 · Bronze
Statuette of a Reclining Woman
Greek, Hellenistic, or Roman · c. 100 BCE–100 CE · amber
Statuette of a Black African Person
Greece, Greco-Roman Period, late Ptolemaic Dynasty · 100–50 BCE · bronze, solid cast, with silver and copper inlays
Dancing Dwarf
Italy(?), Roman · 100 BCE–100 CE · bronze
Tripod Base
Italy, Roman · 100–1 BCE · bronze
Ibis Eating a Lizard
Italy, Rome, Roman Empire · 100 BCE–100 CE · bronze, hollow cast
Fragment: Head of a Man
Greco-Roman · 100 BCE–100 CE · marble
Imperial Eagle
probably Syria, Roman · 100 BCE–100 CE · bronze with garnet inlays
Grave Stele (Relief)
Southern Anatolia, Pamphylia, Hellenistic Greek · c. 50 BCE · marble
Statuette of Harpocrates
Greece, Greco-Roman Period, late Ptolemaic Dynasty · c. 50 BCE · bronze, hollow cast, with silver inlays
Villa of the Mysteries
Roman frescoed villa · c. 60 BCE · Pompeii
Life-size figures on a crimson ground enact what looks like the initiation of a bride into a Dionysian cult — a flagellation, a satyr's mask, a winged figure about to whip a kneeling woman. No one is sure whether the scene is myth, rite, or both.
Oil Lamp
Roman, late Republican-early Imperial (c. 50 BCE – 50 CE) · c. 50 BCE–50 CE · Bronze, cast
Laocoön and His Sons
Hellenistic · 1st c. BCE – 1st c. CE
A Trojan priest and his sons strangled by sea serpents. Dug up in a vineyard in 1506 and hauled to the Vatican. Michelangelo studied it for weeks.
Boy with Thorn
Hellenistic → Roman · 1st c. BCE (later copies)
A boy pulling a thorn from his foot, head bent in total concentration. One of the first ancient bronzes to be widely copied in the Renaissance.
Belvedere Torso
Hellenistic · 1st c. BCE · signed Apollonios
A muscular male torso, seated, armless, headless — and still magnificent. Michelangelo refused to complete it out of reverence; instead he mined it for poses across his ceiling.
Shallow Ribbed Bowl
Roman, Eastern Mediterranean · 50 BCE–100 CE · glass
Statuette of Dionysos
Italy, Roman · 50 BCE–50 CE · bronze and silver and traces of gilding, hollow cast
Cartonnage Mummy Case
Egypt, Greco-Roman period (332 BCE–395 CE), Ptolemaic dynasty (305–30 BCE)–Roman empire (30 BCE–395 CE) · c. 50 BCE–50 CE · cartonnage, painted and gilded, with glass inlays
Attachments Depicting Busts of Silenoi
by Roman
Roman Empire · late republican · -50–50 · Bronze
Portrait Head of C. Cornelius Gallus (?)
Egypt, Greco-Roman period (332 BCE–395 CE), Roman empire (30 BCE–395 CE) · c. 30 BCE · marble
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying Queen Cleopatra VII
by Roman; minted in Eastern Mediterranean
Syria · greco-roman (egyptian) · -37–-33 · Silver
Shekel (Coin) Depicting the God Melkarth
by Phoenician, minted in Tyre, Syria
Tyre · phoenician · -31–-30 · Silver
Head Pendants
by Probably Carthaginian
Carthage · roman period (egyptian) · -30–395 · Glass
Portrait Bust of a Man
Italy, Roman, Augustan period · 27 BCE–14 CE · bronze
Fragment of a Mold for Arretine Bowl
by M. Perennius
Italy, Roman, Augustan period · 27 BCE–14 CE · terracotta
Roman Cameo Glass: Head of Diana
Roman Italy · 1st c. BCE–1st c. CE · cameo glass
A sardonyx-glass cameo with a right-facing Diana — cameo glass imitated the carved layered stone of imperial workshops, letting non-elites wear the look in a ring.
The Vicarello Goblet
Italy, Vicarello (ancient Aquae Apollinares), Roman, Augustan period · 25 BCE–25 CE · silver
Dupondius (Coin) Portraying Germanicus Caesar
by Roman, minted in Rome
Rome · roman (ancient, style or period) · -15–19 · Bronze
Ara Pacis
Augustan Rome · 13–9 BCE
The Altar of Peace. The imperial family processes in carved marble, framed by acanthus tendrils. Augustus inserts himself into the mythic past.
Denarius (Coin) Portraying Emperor Augustus
by Roman; minted in Lugdunum
Italy · roman (ancient, style or period) · -2–4 · Silver

0 – 500 CE

Amphora
Attica · late 6th century BC or first half of 5th century BC
Basket decorated with ribbons
Pompeii · 1st century
Venus with Cupid and doves
Pompeii · 19th century
St Luke painting the Virgin
Crete · Sixteenth Century
Kylix
Attica · late 6th century BC
Watercolour drawing
by James William Wild
Ravenna · 1840s
Earring
Lebanon · 700BC-500BC
Denarius of Marcus Junius Brutus
Roman · 44 BC - 42 BC
Child with a book
by Duquesnoy
Roman · 17th century possibly
Drawing
by Vacher, Sydney
Pompeii
Photograph
by Francis Frith
Pompeii · 1850s to 1870s
Dress
Crete · 1700s
Panel
Crete · 18th century
Roman Millefiori Bowl
Roman Italy · 1st c. CE · glass
A bowl built from cross-sectioned glass canes fused together — the Roman "thousand flowers" technique, lost after antiquity and rediscovered in Renaissance Venice.
Ribbed Glass Bowl
Roman · 1–100 CE · glass
Griffin Atop Entablature
Italy, Pompeii, Roman · 1–100 CE · fresco fragment
Mosaic Pavement
Italy, Roman · 1–100 CE · stone mosaic
Bottle
by Roman; Syria or Palestine
Syria · roman (ancient, style or period) · 1–100 · Glass
Flask in the Shape of a Date
by Roman
Greece · roman (ancient, style or period) · 1–100 · Glass
Pair of Earrings
by Roman; probably Egypt
Roman Empire · greco-roman (egyptian) · 1–100 · Gold and glass
Portrait Bust of the Empress Claudia Octavia
Italy, Roman · 50–70 CE · marble
Cameo: Head of a Woman
Italy, Roman · 1–100 CE · amethyst
Portrait Head of a Youth
Egypt, Alexandria, Roman · 1–100 CE · marble
Togatus Figure
Italy, Roman · 1–200 CE · pentelic marble
Mold for a Male Head
Italy, Roman · 1–300 CE · terracotta
Relief Plaque
by Roman
Rome · imperial (roman) · 1–50 · terracotta
Statue of an Athlete
Italy, Roman · 1–100 CE · marble
Silenus Bust for a Couch
Italy, Roman · 1–125 CE · bronze
Key
Italy, Roman · 1–100 CE · iron tang, bronze handle
Statue of Meleager
by Roman
Roman Empire · roman (ancient, style or period) · 1–199 · Marble
Fountain Spout
by Roman; Pompeii, Italy
Pompeii · roman (ancient, style or period) · 1–100 · Marble
Comic Mask
Greece, Greco-Roman Period, late Ptolemaic Dynasty to Roman Empire · c. 1–199 CE · dark green glass
Intaglio: Victory in a Chariot
Italy, Roman · 1–200 CE · red jasper in gold mount
Maison Carrée
Roman temple · c. 2 CE · Nîmes
The best-preserved Roman temple anywhere, built by Agrippa for the imperial cult. Thomas Jefferson obsessed over its proportions and based the Virginia State Capitol on it, launching the Roman revival in American civic architecture.
Portland Vase
Roman cameo glass · c. 1–25 CE
A cobalt-blue glass vase dipped in white and carved back through the outer layer. One of the finest surviving pieces of Roman cameo glass — and inspiration for Wedgwood's first portraits.
Augustus of Prima Porta
Early Roman Empire · c. 15 CE
The emperor posed as a general, barefoot like a god. The breastplate shows the return of captured Roman standards — a diplomatic win dressed as conquest.
As (Coin) Portraying Drusus
by Roman, minted in Rome
Rome · imperial (roman) · 21–22 · Bronze
Portrait of a Young Girl
Italy, Eastern Roman Empire · c. 25–30 CE · bronze
Funerary Portrait of a Young Girl
Egypt, Roman Empire, late Tiberian · c. 25–37 CE · encaustic on linden wood
Cameo Portraying Emperor Claudius as Jupiter
by Roman; Italy
Italy · imperial (roman) · 41–54 · Cameo: sardonyx Mount: gold
Pont du Gard
Roman aqueduct · 1st century CE · southern France
A three-tiered limestone aqueduct carrying water fifty kilometers to Nîmes across the Gardon valley. Each block weighs up to six tonnes and was set without mortar; the gradient over the full course drops only 12.6 meters.
Ludovisi Gaul
Roman copy after Hellenistic · 1st c. CE
A defeated Galatian warrior plunges a sword into his chest, his dead wife collapsing at his side. Commissioned to commemorate a Pergamene victory, it now reads as an astonishing monument to defeat.
Amphoriskos (Small Amphora)
Roman · 50–100 CE · glass, mold-blown
Canteen or Balsamarium
Roman · 50–250 CE · Bronze, cast
Bowl
Roman, Imperial · c. 100–300 CE · silver: cast and carved
Aryballos (Container for Oil) with Chain
by Roman; Nazareth
Syria · roman (ancient, style or period) · 50–150 · Glass
Statuette of Hercules
by Roman
Roman Empire · Classical · 50–90 · Bronze
Portrait Head of Emperor Vespasian, Recut from Head of Nero
Italy, Roman · 64–79 CE · marble
Aureus (Coin) Portraying Emperor Otho
by Roman; minted in Rome
Rome · imperial (roman) · 69 · Gold
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying Emperor Otho
by Roman, minted in Antioch
Antioch · hellenistic · 69 · Silver
Coin Portraying Emperor Otho
by Roman
Roman Empire · imperial (roman) · 69 · Bronze
Pula Arena
Roman amphitheater · 1st century CE · Istria, Croatia
One of only six surviving Roman amphitheaters that retain a complete outer wall. Seating 23,000 for gladiatorial combat, the limestone ring still hosts opera and concerts under the open sky each summer.
The Colosseum
Flavian · 70–80 CE · Rome
An elliptical amphitheater for 50,000 spectators. The underground hypogeum ran elevators, flooded the floor for sea battles, and housed wild animals.
Pompeii
Roman · buried 79 CE
Vesuvius sealed a whole Roman town in volcanic ash. Frescoes, bread loaves, graffiti, and the shapes of bodies all survived — daily life frozen.
Herculaneum
Roman · buried 79 CE · Italy
Pompeii's smaller, richer neighbor. Volcanic mud sealed it instead of ash, so wooden beams, baby cradles, and carbonized scrolls all survived.
Arch of Titus
Roman Imperial · 81 CE · Rome
Triumphal arch for the sack of Jerusalem. Its inner relief shows the Menorah and other temple spoils being carried in procession — our earliest image of the Second Temple's contents.
Portrait Head of a Man
Italy, Roman · c. 100 CE · marble
Roman Janiform Head Flask
Roman Eastern Mediterranean · 2nd–4th c. CE · mold-blown glass
A blown-glass flask with two faces back-to-back, filled with perfume or unguent — mold-blown in Syria and Palestine, where Roman workshops fed the empire's market in small luxury glass.
Head of Trajan
Italy, Roman · 100–200 CE · marble
Sarcophagus
Italy, Rome, Roman Empire · c. 100–125 CE · Greek marble
Sarcophagus (lid)
Italy, Rome, Roman Empire · c. 100–125 CE · Greek marble
Goblet
Italy, Roman · 100–300 CE · glass
Ring
Roman Empire · 100-400 AD
Pot with Cover
Rhenish (Cologne), Gallo-Roman, 2nd century · 100s CE · tan ware with gray and some brown burnished slip
Pot with Cover (lid)
Rhenish (Cologne), Gallo-Roman, 2nd century · 100–200 CE · tan ware with gray and some brown burnished slip
Jar
by Roman; coast of Syria or Palestine
Mediterranean Region · roman (ancient, style or period) · 101–300 · Glass
Statue of the Aphrodite of Knidos
by Roman
Roman Empire · Classical · 101–200 · Marble
Portrait of a Man Wearing a Laurel Wreath
by Egyptian; The Fayum, Egypt
Al Fayyum · roman period (egyptian) · 101–150 · Lime (linden) wood
Portrait of a Man Wearing an Ivy Wreath
by Egyptian; The Fayum, Egypt
Al Fayyum · roman period (egyptian) · 101–150 · Lime (linden) wood
Fragment of a Portrait Statue of a Man
by Roman
Roman Empire · hellenistic · 101–200 · Marble
Necklace with Pendant
by Roman; probably Egypt
Roman Empire · roman period (egyptian) · 101–300 · Gold
Statue of a Seated Woman
by Roman
Roman Empire · imperial (roman) · 101–200 · Marble
Trajan's Column
Imperial Roman · 113 CE
A 100-foot marble column whose spiral frieze narrates the Dacian Wars in 155 continuous scenes — history carved as a comic strip.
Aureus (Coin) Portraying Emperor Hadrian
by Roman; minted in Rome
Roman Empire · hadrianic · 120–123 · Gold
Head of a Bearded Man
Italy, Roman · c. 125 CE · marble
Library of Celsus at Ephesus
Roman Imperial · 110–135 CE · Turkey
Two-story facade of a library that held 12,000 scrolls and the sarcophagus of its founder in a chamber behind the wall — a library as a tomb.
Pantheon
Roman Imperial · 126 CE · Rome
The largest unreinforced concrete dome ever built. The oculus at its center has been open to the sky for nineteen centuries — rain falls through it into drains in the floor.
Hadrian's Villa
Roman Imperial · 117–138 CE · Tivoli
A 250-acre retreat where the emperor built scale models of the sights from his tour of the empire — a Nile canal, an Athenian stoa, a Canopus.
Apollo Belvedere
Hellenistic / Roman copy · c. 120–140 CE
A marble copy of a lost Greek bronze, long considered the pinnacle of male beauty. Winckelmann helped launch Neoclassicism by writing about this single statue.
Portrait Head of Antinous
by Roman
Italy · imperial (roman) · 130–138 · Marble
Denarius (Coin) Portraying Emperor Hadrian
by Roman, minted in Rome
Roman Empire · imperial (roman) · 134–138 · Silver
Aureus (Coin) Portraying Emperor Antoninus Pius
by Roman; minted in Rome
Roman Empire · imperial (roman) · 138 · Gold
Funerary Portrait of a Woman
Egypt, Roman Empire, Antonine · c. 138–92 CE · encaustic on linen
Portrait Head of a Woman
by Roman
Rome · roman (ancient, style or period) · 140 · marble
Aureus (Coin) Portraying Empress Faustina the Elder
by Roman, minted in Rome
Roman Empire · imperial (roman) · 141–161 · Gold
Sleeping Hermaphroditus
Roman copy of a Greek original · 2nd century CE · Sully Wing
From one angle, a sleeping woman. Walk around to the other side: the body is both. The mattress beneath — realistic enough that you want to touch it — was carved by Bernini in 1619 when the statue was restored.
Portrait Head of a Woman, Probably the Empress Faustina Minor
Italy, Roman · c. 165 CE · marble
Aureus (Coin) Portraying Emperor Marcus Aurelius
by Roman; minted in Rome
Italy · imperial (roman) · 167 · Gold
Equestrian Statue of Marcus Aurelius
Roman Imperial · c. 175 CE
The only surviving large Roman bronze equestrian. It lived because Christians mistook it for Constantine. Now replaced by a copy in the Campidoglio; the original is inside.
Portrait Head of a Noble or Official
Italy, Roman, Antonine period · 175–200 CE · marble
Portonaccio Sarcophagus
Roman · c. 180 CE
A chaotic high-relief battle between Romans and barbarians — tangled bodies, horses, swords, all carved almost in the round. The tomb of an Antonine general; the template for the late Empire's taste for horror vacui.
Column of Marcus Aurelius
Roman · c. 193 CE · Rome
A 30-meter Doric column spiraling 20 reliefs of the emperor's German wars — darker, more harrowing than Trajan's; women and children slaughtered in the corners. A philosopher-king's war memorial.
Leptis Magna
Roman provincial city · expanded 193 CE · Libya
Hometown of Septimius Severus and the showpiece of Roman Africa, with its four-way arch, Severan basilica, and enormous forum paved in imported marble. Desert sand buried and preserved it after the Arab conquest.
Mosaic Floor Panel Depicting Marine Life
by Roman; Daphne, Turkey, the House of the Man of Letters, room 2
Antioch · roman (ancient, style or period) · 200–230 · Stone and mortar
Relief Portrait Head of the Emperor Balbinus
Italy, Roman · c. 238 CE · marble
Oil Ampulla in the Form of a Dancing Bear
Italy, Roman · 200s CE · bronze
Side Panel of a Sarcophagus
by Roman
Antioch · imperial (roman) · 200–250 · Marble
Fragment of a Necklace
by Roman; probably Egypt
Roman Empire · greco-roman (egyptian) · 201–300 · Gold
Denarius (Coin) Portraying Plautilla
by Roman, minted in Rome
Rome · imperial (roman) · 202–205 · Silver
Farnese Hercules
Roman copy after Lysippos · 3rd c. CE
A colossal Hercules leaning exhausted on his club, the apples of the Hesperides clutched behind his back. Found in the Baths of Caracalla; reshaped European ideas of masculine form.
Baths of Caracalla
Roman imperial · 216 CE · Rome
A 25-hectare bathing complex that could hold 1,600 people at once, heated by underfloor hypocausts stoked by armies of slaves. The painted vaults fell centuries ago but Shelley wrote much of Prometheus Unbound wandering the ruins.
Farnese Bull
Roman copy after Hellenistic · 3rd c. CE
The Baths of Caracalla yielded this mountain of marble — the biggest single sculpture to survive antiquity. Two brothers tie their stepmother to a wild bull's horns in Greek myth; rendered in pure Roman excess.
Dish
Italy, Roman · 235 CE · gold and glass
Aureus (Coin) Portraying Emperor Gordian III
by Roman, minted in Rome
Rome · roman (ancient, style or period) · 239 · Gold
Ludovisi Battle Sarcophagus
Late Roman · c. 250 CE
A wall of writhing bodies — Romans hacking through barbarians in high relief, every inch of marble crowded. A late imperial sarcophagus commissioned by a family that commemorated warrior virtue even as Rome was losing.
Portrait Bust of a Girl
Italy, Roman · c. 250–75 CE · marble
Antoninianus (Coin) Portraying Mariniana
by Roman
Rome · roman (ancient, style or period) · 254 · Billon
Coin Portraying King Vabalathus
by Roman
Roman Empire · imperial (roman) · 270–275 · Bronze
Aureus (Coin) Portraying Emperor Aurelian
by Roman, possibly minted in Siscia
Rome · imperial (roman) · 272 · Gold
The Good Shepherd
Anatolia, late Roman-early Christian · 280–90 CE · marble
Portrait Bust of an Aristocratic Man
Anatolia, late Roman-early Christian · 280–90 CE · marble
Portrait Bust of an Aristocratic Woman
Anatolia, late Roman-early Christian · 280–90 CE · marble
Portrait of the Four Tetrarchs
Late Roman · c. 300 CE
Porphyry sculpture of Diocletian and his three co-emperors embracing in pairs. Features deliberately alike, eyes blank, proportions stocky — not portraits but a statement: the office is the man.
Lod Mosaic
Roman · c. 300 CE · Israel
A 180-square-meter floor mosaic of African and marine animals — lions, elephants, ducks, a giraffe and rhino, fish and seahorses. Discovered under a highway construction in 1996, untouched by the house that once stood on it.
Pitcher with Handle
Roman, Syro-Palestinian · 300s CE or later · glass, mold-blown
Mosaic of Tigress and Cubs
Eastern Roman Empire · 300s CE · tesserae
Cup
Eastern Mediterranean, Roman · c. 300–400 CE · glass
Mosaic Fragment with Grazing Camel
by Byzantine; Eastern Mediterranean, probably Syria
Byzantine Empire · byzantine · 301–500 · Stone in mortar
Kuttrolf (Bottle with Divided Neck)
by Late Roman or Byzantine; possibly Eastern Mediterranean
Syria · roman (ancient, style or period) · 301–400 · Glass
Diocletian's Palace
Late Roman · 305 CE · Split, Croatia
A retired emperor's fortified seaside retreat built on the plan of a military camp. Its peristyle, mausoleum (now the cathedral), and substructures form the kernel around which the medieval city of Split grew.
Arch of Constantine
Late Roman · 315 CE · Rome
A triumphal arch cobbled together from reused second-century reliefs of Trajan, Hadrian, and Marcus Aurelius — a frank admission that fourth-century sculptors could no longer match the earlier skill. The recycled figures were even recut with Constantine's head.
Coin Portraying Emperor Constantine II or Emperor Constantius Gallus
by Roman
Roman Empire · constantinian · 317–354 · Bronze
Solidus (Coin) Portraying Emperor Constantine I
by Roman; minted in Antioch
Antioch · imperial (roman) · 324–325 · Gold
Late Roman Spoon with Saint Paul
Late Roman · 4th c. CE · silver and niello
A silver spoon inscribed with Paul in a victor's pose — by the 4th century Roman silversmiths were recasting saints as athletes of the faith, blending pagan and Christian at table.
Bracelet
Syria, Roman · 350–500 CE · glass
Sarcophagus of Junius Bassus
Early Christian · 359 CE
A Roman prefect's sarcophagus carved with ten Old and New Testament scenes — one of the earliest substantial works of Christian art made by people who had ever seen a classical sculptor's studio.
Fork with Mule-Head Finial
Italy, Roman · 375–425 CE · silver
Missorium of Theodosius
Late Roman · c. 388 CE
A huge silver presentation plate showing the emperor enthroned between his co-rulers, handing a diploma to an officer. The late-imperial style already Byzantine — frontal, hierarchical, strict.
Mosaic Fragment with Man Leading a Giraffe
by Byzantine; Syria or Lebanon
Tyre · byzantine · 401–500 · Stone in mortar

500 – 1000

Altar Front
early Byzantium, Constantinople or Ravenna, Byzantine period, 6th century · c. 540–600 · marble
Mosaic head
Ravenna (city) · ca. 545

1000 – 1500

The Massacre of the Innocents
by Marco Dente da Ravenna
Italy · 1408–1560 · Engraving on paper
Icon of the Mother of God and Infant Christ (Virgin Eleousa)
by Angelos Akotantos
Republic of Venice, Kingdom of Candia, Candia (Heraklion, Crete, Greece) or Byzantine Empire, Constantinople (Istanbul, Turkey) · c. 1425–50 · tempera and gold on wood

1500 – 1800

Satyr Mother and Child
by Workshop of Severo da Ravenna
Italy · sixteenth century · 1510–1540 · Bronze with dark brown patina
Courage
by Marco Dente da Ravenna
Italy · 1511–1525 · Engraving in black ink on paper
Juno, Ceres, and Psyche
by Marco Dente da Ravenna
Italy · 1513–1523 · Engraving on paper
Saint Thomas
by Marco Dente da Ravenna
Italy · 1515–1527 · Engraving
Throne of Neptune
by Marco Dente da Ravenna
Italy · 1519 · Engraving in black on cream laid paper
God Commanding Noah to Build the Ark
by Marco Dente da Ravenna
Italy · 1520 · Engraving in black on tan laid paper
Cameo Portraying Tiberius
by Roman
Italy · imperial (roman) · 1525–1550 · Gold
The Two Armies at the Battle of Ravenna, 1512
by Master with the Mousetrap
Italy · 1530 · Engraving in black on paper
Border
Crete · 1700-1800
Skirt border
by Grammatiko, Georgia
Crete · 1733

1800 onward

Out of Rome - Church of S. Vitale at Ravenna. Part of the Choir, shewing arches and capitals ornamented with Mosaics, A.D. 550.
by Parker, John Henry
Italy · 1864-1870
One of 235 drawings of architectural subjects in France, Germany, and Italy
by Millard, Walter John Nash
